Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Negative Appraisals.value: You can never know who will harm you

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I remind myself it could have been worse
I dwell on what other people have done to me

vignette:
She is an 80-year-old European widow who presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following a life-threatening event. Her distress is reflected in a very high PCL-5 score of 78. In day-to-day life she describes being constantly on guard, sleeping badly, and experiencing intense physical reactions when something reminds her of the event. A burnt smell is especially unsettling for her, and reminders involving related people or any sense of not being in control can quickly leave her tense and frightened. She appears exhausted by the effort of getting through ordinary routines.

Her view of the world has narrowed into harsh and fearful expectations. She speaks as though danger may come from anyone, saying that you can never know who will harm you. Alongside this sits a strong sense of personal failure, with repeated thoughts that she is a weak person and that she has no future. These beliefs seem to colour the way she interprets daily experiences, leaving her more watchful and more easily thrown into a state of alarm.

In response, she relies on coping patterns that bring only limited relief. She avoids the site of the event, dwells on what other people have done to her, and at times tries to put matters in perspective by reminding herself that it could have been worse. These strategies seem to offer brief distance from distress, but they also keep her life restricted and her attention fixed on the trauma and its meaning.

The overall picture is one of persistent fear and narrowing of activity. She is hypervigilant, sleep is unsettled, and ordinary cues can trigger strong bodily arousal. Her relationships appear strained by suspicion and preoccupation, and the loss of safety has left her feeling vulnerable and alone. Despite her attempts to manage, the combination of threat-related reminders, harsh self-appraisal, and repetitive coping leaves her stuck in a cycle of ongoing distress.
